items.myth: Pinning your direct dependencies is enough for reproducibility.
items.reality: Transitive dependencies can still drift; a lockfile pins the entire resolved tree, not just the packages you named.
items.example: `requirements.txt: fastapi==0.110.0` is one line. The lockfile that backs it has ~40 lines — `pydantic`, `starlette`, `anyio`, `sniffio`, `typing-extensions`, ... each pinned. Without those pins, any one of them can break you overnight.

items.myth: A lockfile and a manifest are the same thing.
items.reality: The manifest expresses loose intent you edit; the lockfile is a generated, exact resolution you commit but don't hand-edit.
items.example: `pyproject.toml` says `httpx = '^0.27'` (the contract). `poetry.lock` says `httpx==0.27.2 + 6 transitives + hashes` (the realisation). One declares, the other captures the resolution.

items.reality: They freeze versions until you deliberately re-lock; they enable controlled updates, not permanent stasis.
items.example: Monthly cadence: `poetry update httpx` (or `uv lock --upgrade-package httpx`) bumps just that package and re-resolves the tree. The lock changes intentionally, in a reviewable PR.
